summary: This guest post by mathematician Matilde Marcolli challenges the Silicon Valley monopoly on futurist thinking by tracing its themes back to early 20th century Russian Futurism and Cosmism. She argues that the crucial difference between capitalist and anarchist futurism isn't the topics they explore—immortality, space, AI—but whose future they envision. The post introduces Anarcho-Transhumanism as an anti-capitalist alternative that embraces technology for liberation rather than elite preservation. Marcolli highlights 'morphological freedom' as a key concept, emphasizing transgender rights and disability rights over botox treatments for billionaires. She concludes that science and technology have enormous potential when used inclusively rather than for profit and exploitation.
key_points: Silicon Valley futurism's core themes (digital immortality, space colonization, AI superintelligence) all originated in Russian Futurism and Cosmism a century ago
The essential distinction between futurisms is not their subject matter but their vision of who benefits—wealthy elites or all of humanity
Anarcho-Transhumanism offers an anti-capitalist, pro-technology alternative rooted in movements like Afrofuturism, Cyberfeminism, and Xenofeminism
Morphological freedom in anarchist thinking prioritizes transgender rights and challenging ableism, not luxury enhancements for the wealthy
Grassroots initiatives like biohacker spaces and DIY synthetic biology represent technology serving liberation rather than capital
money_quotes: It is easy to laugh off all these ideas as the typical preoccupations of a group of aging narcissist wealthy white males, whose greatest fear is that an artificial intelligence may one day treat them the way they have been treating everybody else all along.

The crucial difference in these forms of futurism does not lie in the themes of speculation, but rather in the role of humanity in this envisioned future. Is this the future of a wealthy elite? Is this the future of a classless society?
The Vitruvian Man, which was the very symbol of Humanism, was also a symbol of the intrinsically exclusionary nature of Humanism.
When children are given the possibility to design and 3D print their own prosthetics, they make colorful arms that launch darts and flying saucers and that make them look like superheroes.
Science and technology have enormous potential, if used inclusively and for the benefit of all and not with goals of profit and exploitation.
key_insight: The question that defines any futurism isn't what technologies it imagines, but whose liberation those technologies are meant to serve.
